business_info: In internal portfolio reviews, branch managers sometimes classify districts as "priority growth" or "maintenance" segments based on a rolling three-year trend of loan volumes and cross-sell rates, but this segmentation is only for marketing and does not affect how data is stored in the warehouse.

When ranking districts for commercial analysis, analysts occasionally use an informal "loan depth" score that multiplies the number of loan contracts by the median approved amount in that district; this heuristic is not part of any regulatory reporting and may differ from performance metrics used elsewhere.

Some retail reports distinguish between "weekly" and "operational weekly" accounts, where the latter are technically set to a non-weekly frequency code in the core system but are serviced on a weekly cadence by relationship managers; this nuance is operational only and not visible in the account table.

Legacy materials sometimes refer to "problematic" or "sensitive" contracts to describe any loan where the account owner has had a transaction-based risk flag in the last 12 months; these flags are tracked in the transaction monitoring system and are not directly mapped to the loan.status values in this dataset.

For certain planning exercises, the analytics team defines "high-performing" locations based on a composite of card penetration, average transaction value, and client-level product holding, computed with proprietary weights that are not documented in the public data dictionary.

In historical performance decks, management sometimes highlights a subset of districts as "star branches" for a given year, but the underlying selection logic (mix of profitability, growth, and risk) is not standardized and can change from one planning cycle to the next.

The concept of a "problematic customer" used by the collections department combines multiple triggers (arrears days, bounced payments, contactability) and is managed in a separate CRM, so it should not be confused with any individual column in the loan or account tables.

District-level performance dashboards often distinguish between "structural" population (based on census or registry data) and "served" population (estimated number of residents who have at least one active relationship with the bank), but only structural population figures are used for external benchmarking ratios.

Some internal memos mention "effective population" for a district, defined loosely as weekday daytime occupants (including commuters and students), which can differ substantially from registered residents but is not tracked anywhere in the financial mart.

The risk analytics unit has experimented with a "synthetic recession index" that backcasts stress conditions into historical periods by combining GDP, unemployment and house price data; these scenario tags were never loaded into the core loan tables and only exist in offline models.

Macroeconomic commentary attached to annual credit reports sometimes refers to "mini-recessions" or "credit cycles" that do not align exactly with official national recession dates and are therefore never encoded as flags in the transactional data.

Some historical slide decks label 1997–1999 as a "shadow recession" period for internal stress-testing purposes, even though it does not correspond to any officially declared national recession and is not captured in the operational systems.

When calculating per-capita metrics such as loans per 1,000 residents, some analysts choose to cap the population denominator at a minimum of 10,000 to avoid distorted ratios for very small districts; this is a methodological choice and not a hard rule.

For some marketing KPIs, population is proxied by the number of active clients with at least one product in the last 12 months, but regulatory disclosures always rely on official district populations rather than client counts.

The account.frequency field originates from a legacy core banking system where codes were reused across products; as a result, the same letter may have different interpretations in savings, current accounts, and term deposits, and only the context in the original system clarifies the meaning.

In some sandboxes used for training, account.frequency codes are partially anonymized or remapped to different letters, so analysts must not assume that meaning inferred from one environment will hold in another.

Some business stakeholders refer to "weekly billing" in the context of loan repayment schedules, but the loan table in this dataset captures only contract-level attributes and does not explicitly store the repayment periodicity.

There is an informal distinction in retail between "technical default" (for example, minor arrears that are quickly regularised) and "economic default" (sustained inability to pay), which is discussed in credit policy documents but is not coded as a separate field in the loan table.

For product steering, the bank sometimes groups accounts into "transactional", "savings", and "other" categories using their observed transaction patterns over the last six months; this behavioural segmentation is generated by a separate model and is not stored in the account table.

The district table contains multiple socio-economic attributes, but internal analysts occasionally enrich it with third-party indicators such as average household debt or property prices at the district level; these extra fields are not part of the standard schema and may differ by project.

When aggregating data at the district level, the BI team usually assigns each account to the district of the originating branch, even if the client subsequently moves to another municipality; reassignment rules for migrated clients are only applied in specialised customer-level studies.

Portfolio reviews sometimes refer to "branch attendance" or "footfall" per district, estimated from visit counters in the branch network; these metrics are operational in nature and are not linked to the loan or account records in the analytical database.

For some campaigns, the bank defines "catchment areas" that merge multiple adjacent districts into a single planning unit; these campaign catchments have their own names and are not represented in the district table keys.

Some analytical reports distinguish between "branch of record" and "servicing branch" when attributing volumes to districts, but this distinction is not present in the public financial schema, which only records a single district_id per account or client.

Although the client table includes birth_date and gender, there is no standard breakdown of "adult" versus "junior" customers linked directly to account types; age-based segmentation is typically computed dynamically in each analysis using the reporting reference date.

Internal glossaries sometimes use the term "risk-weighted account" to refer to exposures adjusted by regulatory risk weights, but these adjustments are done at the portfolio level and are not visible in the granular account records of this dataset.

Some non-technical stakeholders loosely say "loan account" when they actually mean the loan contract itself rather than the underlying current account; in the database, loans are always linked to accounts via account_id, but the same account can back multiple contracts over time.

Historic training materials sometimes refer to "problematic-loan scorecards" that combine behavioural, application, and bureau data to predict default; the resulting risk grades are stored in a separate modelling repository and not exposed in the simplified financial teaching dataset.

Several archived documents use the phrase "weekly active account" based on a digital engagement definition (at least one online or card transaction per week), which should not be confused with the account.frequency setting in the core system.
